question_text: In a crossing situation between two power-driven vessels, the give-way vessel should normally:
option_b: Avoid crossing ahead if circumstances allow

question_text: Under COLREG Rule 5, a proper lookout must be maintained by:
option_b: The master only
option_c: Sight and hearing as well as all available means

question_text: A vessel constrained by her draught is displaying by day:
option_b: A black cylinder

question_text: In restricted visibility, the safe speed of a vessel should be:
option_b: Adjusted to the prevailing circumstances and conditions
option_c: Set only by the engine manufacturer
option_d: The same as in clear weather if radar is operational

question_text: A sailing vessel underway at night should show sidelights and:
option_b: A white all-round light

question_text: When two power-driven vessels are meeting head-on, each should:
option_b: Maintain course until close-quarters develops

question_text: The stand-on vessel in a crossing situation should initially:
option_b: Maintain course and speed
option_c: Alter to port to help the give-way vessel

question_text: A vessel not under command shows at night:
option_a: Two all-round red lights in a vertical line
option_b: Three all-round green lights in a vertical line

question_text: The sound signal for a power-driven vessel underway but stopped and making no way in fog is:
option_b: Two prolonged blasts every 2 minutes
option_c: One short, one prolonged, one short

question_text: Under Rule 10, a vessel using a traffic separation lane should normally:
option_a: Proceed against the direction of traffic if clear
option_b: Keep as near to the outer limit as practicable
option_c: Proceed in the general direction of traffic flow

question_text: A vessel engaged in pilotage duty on pilotage duty may show at night:
option_b: White over white lights

question_text: When overtaking, a vessel is deemed to be overtaking if coming up from more than:
option_b: 10 degrees abaft the beam

question_text: A vessel at anchor of 50 metres or more shall exhibit by day:
option_b: Two black balls

question_text: Which light is carried by a towing vessel when the tow exceeds 200 metres?
option_a: Two masthead lights in a vertical line
option_b: Three masthead lights in a vertical line
option_c: A red over white over red vertical line

question_text: Action taken to avoid collision should be:
option_b: Positive, made in ample time, and with due regard to good seamanship
option_c: Delayed until the last safe moment

question_text: The chart symbol 'Fl(2) 8s' indicates:
option_a: A fixed light for 2 seconds every 8 seconds
option_b: A group of two flashes every 8 seconds
option_c: Two lights flashing alternately over 8 seconds
option_d: A flashing light visible for 8 miles

question_text: A true bearing is measured:
option_b: Clockwise from true north

question_text: The main purpose of a large-scale chart in pilotage waters is to:
option_b: Provide detailed information for close navigation
option_c: Replace the need for visual lookout

question_text: A line joining positions of equal depth is called:
option_b: An isobar

question_text: On an Admiralty chart, a magenta dashed line commonly indicates:
option_a: A traffic lane or routeing feature
option_b: A depth contour
option_c: A submarine cable out of use

question_text: The best charted objects for a visual fix are those that are:
option_b: Conspicuous, accurately charted, and well separated
option_d: Close together on the same bearing

question_text: A position obtained from a single object and radar range is usually called a:
option_b: Dead reckoning position

question_text: When transferring a position line to obtain a running fix, the line is moved by the vessel's:
option_b: Course and distance made good between observations

question_text: The chart datum is primarily used as the reference for:
option_b: Charted depths and heights of tide

question_text: A danger bearing is mainly used to:
option_b: Indicate a line that should not be crossed

question_text: A clearing bearing is used to:
option_a: Keep a vessel clear of a danger
option_b: Measure current speed
option_c: Find range off by vertical sextant angle

question_text: The abbreviation 'PA' on a chart usually means:
option_b: Pilot aboard

question_text: An isolated danger mark has topmarks consisting of:
option_b: Two black cones points together

question_text: On a Mercator chart, rhumb lines are represented as:
option_b: Great circles

question_text: A chart note stating 'positions in this area are unreliable' should lead the navigator to:
option_b: Use extra caution and cross-check with all available means
option_c: Avoid the area in all circumstances

question_text: The first objective when preparing a berth-to-berth passage plan is to:
option_b: Appraise all relevant information

question_text: During the appraisal stage of passage planning, the navigator should consider:
option_a: Charts, publications, weather, tides, and vessel limitations
option_b: Only the departure chart

question_text: A wheel-over position is marked on the plan mainly to indicate where to:
option_b: Commence alteration of course

question_text: No-go areas on a passage plan are intended to show:
option_b: Water where the vessel must not enter safely

question_text: Under-keel clearance policy on a passage plan should account for:
option_b: Static draught only
option_c: Squat, heel, tide, and chart accuracy

question_text: When a pilot boards, the bridge team should:
option_a: Hand over navigation entirely and leave the bridge
option_b: Continue an effective bridge team and monitor the pilotage plan
option_c: Stop fixing the vessel until alongside

question_text: The most suitable method for checking progress in a narrow channel is:
option_b: Frequent positions by all appropriate means with clear cross-track limits
option_c: A single fix at the channel entrance
option_d: Relying on the pilot's local knowledge alone

question_text: Parallel indexing on radar is primarily used to:
option_b: Monitor cross-track distance from a charted line or object

question_text: A planned position at a stated time on the passage plan is often called:
option_b: A waypoint

question_text: A call to the master should be made when:
option_a: The OOW is in any doubt about the situation
option_b: The sea is calm and visibility is good

question_text: When entering a traffic separation scheme, the passage plan should include:
option_b: Likely reporting points and rule-compliant routeing

question_text: The purpose of a contingency plan within passage planning is to:
option_b: Provide actions for reasonably foreseeable emergencies or deviations
option_d: Avoid notifying the master of problems

question_text: Before commencing pilotage, the bridge team should normally agree:
option_a: Only the pilot's preferred VHF channel
option_b: The detailed plan, wheel-over points, abort points, and UKC limits

question_text: An abort point in a pilotage plan is the position beyond which:
option_b: The intended manoeuvre should not be continued without commitment
option_c: The vessel must increase to full speed

question_text: When monitoring a passage plan, a repeated cross-track error trend usually means:
option_a: The chart scale is too large
option_b: The vessel is consistently being set or steered off track

question_text: On a relative-motion radar display, a target with zero relative movement is likely to be:
option_b: On a steady bearing with risk of collision if range is decreasing
option_c: Anchored to the seabed in all cases
option_d: Clear because its true course is shown

question_text: Sea clutter on radar is mainly caused by:
option_a: Returns from waves near the ship
option_b: Electrical interference from the gyro repeater

question_text: The anti-rain clutter control is intended to reduce echoes from:
option_b: Precipitation

question_text: A 3 cm radar compared with a 10 cm radar generally gives:
option_b: Better definition of small targets at short range

question_text: The main limitation of radar for collision avoidance is that it:
option_b: May not show the intentions of other vessels

question_text: An off-centre display on radar is most useful when:
option_a: Looking well ahead on the planned track
option_b: Checking the gyro error

question_text: On a head-up stabilized display, a target echo abaft the beam will:
option_b: Move with alterations of own ship's heading

question_text: Blind sectors on radar are dangerous because they:
option_b: Can prevent detection of targets in certain bearings

question_text: The best way to confirm that a weak radar contact is real is to:
option_b: Observe it on successive sweeps and settings, and cross-check if possible
option_d: Change only the display colour palette

question_text: The variable range marker on radar is used primarily to:
option_b: Assess target aspect visually

question_text: Electronic bearing lines on radar are used to:
option_b: Reduce sea clutter

question_text: ARPA trial manoeuvre is used to:
option_b: Predict the effect of a proposed avoiding action

question_text: A tracked ARPA target showing a very small CPA and short TCPA requires:
option_a: No action if AIS name is known
option_b: Immediate appraisal and, if necessary, early avoiding action
option_d: A change to hand steering regardless of risk

question_text: ARPA information is most reliable when:
option_a: Targets are tracked for sufficient time with accurate sensor inputs
option_b: Own ship is rolling heavily
option_d: The radar is left on automatic gain only

question_text: A target vector on true motion radar represents the target's:
option_b: Compass error
option_c: Motion over the ground or water as selected

question_text: The primary advantage of ECDIS in route monitoring is:
option_a: It removes the need for a lookout
option_b: It can provide automatic alarms related to the planned route

question_text: Before relying on an ENC, the navigator should confirm the:
option_b: Dataset is appropriate, up to date, and correctly loaded

question_text: An ECDIS safety contour is mainly used to:
option_b: Highlight depths shallower than a chosen limit

question_text: The term 'overscale' on ECDIS means:
option_a: The display is being viewed at a larger scale than the data was compiled for
option_b: The ship is moving too fast for the screen refresh
option_d: The route contains too many waypoints

question_text: An ECDIS alarm should never be:
option_b: Permanently silenced without understanding the cause
option_c: Recorded when required by company procedures

question_text: What is the purpose of setting a cross-track limit in ECDIS?
option_b: To warn if the vessel deviates excessively from the planned track
option_d: To change the ship's heading automatically

question_text: A route check in ECDIS should be carried out:
option_b: Before execution and whenever the route is amended
option_c: Only by the master in port
option_d: Only if a pilot is not embarked

question_text: The SCAMIN attribute on ENC objects affects:
option_a: The minimum display scale at which objects appear
option_b: The sound signal for a fog signal station

question_text: When an ECDIS position source is lost, the OOW should first:
option_a: Ignore the alarm if visual bearings are available
option_b: Assess the situation and continue fixing by alternative means
option_d: Delete the route and rebuild it

question_text: Raster chart display differs from ENC display because raster charts:
option_a: Are object-based and alarm-enabled by default
option_b: Are digital pictures of paper charts
option_c: Do not require any official updates
option_d: Always show more depth detail than ENCs

question_text: The conning display on an integrated bridge system is mainly intended to show:
option_b: Essential navigation and manoeuvring data in one place

question_text: A gyrocompass seeks the direction of:
option_b: Grid north
option_d: Compass north corrected for deviation only

question_text: Compass deviation is caused mainly by:
option_b: Local magnetic influence from the ship

question_text: Magnetic variation is the angle between:
option_b: True north and magnetic north
option_d: Gyro north and compass north only when rolling

question_text: A vessel on easterly compass error will steer a compass course that is:
option_a: Less than the true course for the same intended heading
option_b: More than the true course for the same intended heading
option_c: Always equal to the magnetic course

question_text: The azimuth of a celestial body may be used to determine:
option_b: Compass error

question_text: The amplitude of the sun is taken when the sun is:
option_b: On the prime vertical

question_text: A gyrocompass error may be checked by comparison with a:
option_b: Transit bearing of charted objects

question_text: If the gyro repeater differs significantly from the master gyro, the OOW should:
option_a: Ignore it if the track looks correct
option_b: Treat it as a defect and use verified heading information

question_text: In high latitudes, gyrocompass performance may:
option_a: Improve markedly due to convergence of meridians
option_b: Become less reliable

question_text: The coefficient B on a deviation card is associated mainly with:
option_b: Quadrantal deviation

question_text: A magnetic compass heeling error is most noticeable when the vessel:
option_b: Is upright on an even keel

question_text: The lubber line of a magnetic compass indicates:
option_b: The ship's fore-and-aft line

question_text: A gyrocompass requires latitude input primarily to correct for:
option_b: Earth rate effects

question_text: The standard magnetic compass should be located where it is:
option_b: Subject to minimum magnetic interference

question_text: When converting true course to compass course, the navigator applies:
option_b: Deviation then variation

question_text: The height of tide at a given time is found by applying the:
option_b: Tidal prediction for the place and time

question_text: A spring tide occurs when:
option_a: The sun and moon act approximately in line
option_b: There is no moon
option_c: The moon is at first quarter only

question_text: Neap tides occur when the sun and moon are:
option_b: At right angles relative to the Earth

question_text: The interval between the moon's meridian passage and local high water is called the:
option_b: Chart datum allowance

question_text: A tidal stream atlas is mainly used to determine:
option_b: Direction and rate of tidal streams at stated times

question_text: When calculating secondary port height of tide, the navigator uses:
option_b: Differences applied to the standard port data
option_c: Only the standard port times without correction

question_text: Charted depths are reduced to chart datum because chart datum is usually:
option_b: A low-water reference level
option_c: The mean sea level at all ports
option_d: Equal to mean high water springs

question_text: A flood stream is the stream that:
option_b: Sets towards high water or upstream

question_text: If a vessel experiences a set to starboard, the current is moving the vessel:
option_a: To port of the intended track
option_b: To starboard of the intended track

question_text: Slack water is the period when:
option_a: There is no tide height change
option_b: The tidal stream is at or near zero rate
option_d: The vessel is not making way

question_text: The main purpose of calculating squat is to assess:
option_b: Reduction in under-keel clearance due to speed in shallow water

question_text: In confined shallow water, squat generally increases as:
option_b: Speed decreases

question_text: If the charted depth is 11.0 m, the height of tide is 2.4 m, and squat is 0.4 m, the available water depth is:
A tidal diamond on a chart gives information about:
option_b: Tidal stream direction and rate
option_c: Height of tide at springs only

question_text: A veering wind changes direction:
option_b: Counter-clockwise

question_text: In the northern hemisphere, wind circulates around a low-pressure system generally:
option_b: Anticlockwise and inward

question_text: A rapid fall in barometric pressure may indicate:
option_a: Approaching bad weather or a depression
option_b: Immediate improvement in visibility
option_d: Slack water at the next port

question_text: Advection fog forms when:
option_a: Warm moist air passes over a colder surface
option_b: The sky is cloudless overnight over land
option_c: A cold front passes with thunderstorms
option_d: Rain evaporates into dry air aloft only

question_text: A backing wind changes direction:
option_b: Counter-clockwise

question_text: The symbol on a synoptic chart with triangles on one side represents a:
option_b: Cold front

question_text: In the northern hemisphere, Buys Ballot's law states that with the wind on your back, low pressure lies to your:
option_b: Right

question_text: The main hazard of cumulonimbus cloud to a vessel is:
option_b: Squalls, thunderstorms, and poor visibility

question_text: Wave period is the time between:
option_a: Two successive wave crests passing a fixed point
option_b: High water and low water

question_text: A swell differs from a sea because swell is:
option_a: Generated locally by present wind only
option_b: Usually more regular and has travelled from a distant source

question_text: An occluded front forms when:
option_a: A warm front overtakes a cold front
option_b: A cold front overtakes a warm front

question_text: The dew point is the temperature at which air becomes:
option_b: Saturated and condensation may begin

question_text: The Beaufort scale describes wind force based originally on:
option_b: Observed sea and weather conditions

question_text: A line joining places of equal atmospheric pressure is called an:
option_b: Isotherm

question_text: A ridge on a weather chart is an extension of:
option_b: High pressure

question_text: The officer of the watch should not hand over the watch if:
option_b: A manoeuvre is in progress or a danger is developing

question_text: A proper bridge watch at sea should normally include:
option_b: A lookout maintained as required in addition to the OOW when necessary

question_text: The bridge navigational watch alarm system is designed to:
option_b: Monitor alertness on the bridge and call assistance if needed

question_text: When visibility deteriorates, the OOW should first:
option_b: Call the master, post extra lookout as required, and proceed at safe speed
option_c: Maintain full speed until traffic is seen

question_text: The helmsman should repeat and confirm helm orders:
option_b: Clearly and immediately
option_c: Only when the order is unusual
option_d: Never if hand steering is engaged

question_text: When changing from automatic to hand steering, the bridge team should:
option_b: Ensure the changeover is checked and understood

question_text: The OOW's watchkeeping responsibilities include:
option_a: Keeping the ship on schedule at all costs
option_b: Maintaining safe navigation, lookout, and compliance with regulations
option_c: Managing cargo calculations during every watch
option_d: Delegating all collision avoidance to the AB

question_text: A lookout should report immediately:
option_b: Any lights, shapes, sound signals, or hazards detected

question_text: If the bridge team becomes uncertain of the vessel's position, the safest response is to:
option_a: Continue at planned speed until the next fix
option_b: Reduce risk, call the master, and establish position by all available means
option_d: Rely solely on the ECDIS route line

question_text: Night order books are intended primarily to:
option_b: Record the master's standing instructions for the bridge team

question_text: Fatigue is a navigation risk mainly because it can:
option_b: Degrade lookout, judgment, and response time

question_text: The OOW should ensure navigation lights are:
option_a: Checked and confirmed operational at the appropriate times
option_b: Switched on only when another ship is sighted

question_text: When steering by magnetic compass after gyro failure, the bridge team should be especially alert to:
option_b: Compass error and steering accuracy

question_text: A master's standing order to call him if in doubt means the OOW should:
option_a: Wait until the doubt becomes an emergency
option_b: Call promptly whenever uncertainty affects safe navigation
option_c: Call only after consulting the chief engineer
option_d: Record the doubt and mention it at handover

question_text: The main reason for monitoring course recorder and heading repeaters is to:
option_b: Confirm the vessel is being steered as intended

question_text: If a vessel steers 090°T for 2 hours at 12 knots with no set or drift, the distance made good is:
option_b: 12 miles

question_text: A vessel runs 45 miles in 3 hours. Her average speed is:
option_b: 15 knots

question_text: If ETA must be advanced by 1 hour over a remaining distance of 120 miles, the required increase in speed from 12 knots is to:
option_b: 14 knots

question_text: The main purpose of a back bearing in pilotage is to:
option_a: Check the vessel remains on a transit or planned line after passing an object
option_b: Determine tidal height

question_text: A transit is most useful because it provides:
option_a: A line of position with high directional accuracy
option_b: A direct measure of tide height

question_text: When approaching a berth with a strong crosswind, the bridge team should be especially aware of:
option_b: Leeway and available manoeuvring room

question_text: A conspicuous sector light is especially valuable in pilotage because it:
option_a: Shows the exact depth under the keel
option_b: Indicates whether the vessel is within a safe sector

question_text: An echo sounder is most useful during pilotage for:
option_a: Providing a continuous check on depth trends against expectation
option_b: Determining magnetic variation

question_text: When anchoring in a tidal stream, the vessel should normally be brought to the anchor with:
option_b: The current ahead and the vessel under control

question_text: A position circle from a radar range touches a position line at a very small angle. The resulting fix is likely to be:
option_b: Poorly conditioned and less reliable

question_text: The most reliable immediate check on a suspected GNSS error near land is to:
option_b: Cross-check with independent visual, radar, and depth information

question_text: The term 'set' refers to the current's:
option_b: Direction toward which it flows
option_c: Direction from which the wind blows

question_text: The term 'drift' refers to the current's:
option_b: Direction from which it flows

question_text: A vessel making good less speed over the ground than through the water is likely experiencing:
option_b: A head current

question_text: If the true course is 210°, variation 4°W, and deviation 2°E, the compass course is:
A relative bearing of 045° on the starboard bow from a ship heading 120°T gives a true bearing of:
On a course-up display, the top of the screen represents:
option_b: The vessel's current course or heading reference

question_text: The main purpose of an under-keel clearance alarm in ECDIS or an IBS is to:
option_b: Give timely warning that safe depth margins may be reducing
option_d: Show squat directly from engine load

question_text: Before entering a narrow channel in reduced visibility, the bridge team should ensure:
option_a: Radar, sound signals, engines, and lookout arrangements are ready
option_b: Only the pilot ladder is rigged
option_c: Only the ECDIS route is displayed north-up

question_text: The best description of good bridge resource management is:
option_a: One person navigating alone to avoid confusion
option_b: Using all available people, information, and equipment effectively for safe navigation
option_c: Allowing the pilot to override all monitoring
option_d: Keeping discussions to a minimum even when concerned
